quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Work by variable force"?

options.text: W = ∫ₐᵇ F(x)dx; the integral of force over displacement gives total work done (in joules or foot-pounds)
Speeding up: v(t) and a(t) have the same sign; slowing down: they have opposite signs
The + C in every indefinite integral accounts for the fact that F(x) + C has the same derivative for any constant C
f'(x) > 0: f is increasing (quantity growing); f'(x) < 0: f is decreasing (quantity declining)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Work by variable force" is defined as: W = ∫ₐᵇ F(x)dx; the integral of force over displacement gives total work done (in joules or foot-pounds). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Hooke's Law"?

options.text: Use a constraint equation to express the objective function in terms of one variable before finding critical points
G = 2∫₀¹ [x - L(x)]dx; ranges from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality); measures income distribution
If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0: local minimum (concave up); f''(c) < 0: local maximum (concave down)
F = kx for a spring; work to stretch from 0 to x: W = ∫₀ˣ kx dx = ½kx²

quiz_questions.explanation: "Hooke's Law" is defined as: F = kx for a spring; work to stretch from 0 to x: W = ∫₀ˣ kx dx = ½kx². The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Pumping work"?

options.text: d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n; includes x⁻¹ = 1/x, x^(1/2) = √x, x^(2/3), etc.
If f' changes from + to - at c: local maximum; from - to +: local minimum; no sign change: neither
∫csc²x dx = -cot x + C; ∫sec x tan x dx = sec x + C; ∫csc x cot x dx = -csc x + C
W = ∫₀ᴴ ρgA(y)·y dy; the work to pump water from a tank, where A(y) is the cross-sectional area at height y

quiz_questions.explanation: "Pumping work" is defined as: W = ∫₀ᴴ ρgA(y)·y dy; the work to pump water from a tank, where A(y) is the cross-sectional area at height y. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Center of mass (1D)"?

options.text: If f'(x) = 0 for all x in (a,b), then f is constant on [a,b]; proven using MVT
x̄ = (Σmᵢxᵢ)/(Σmᵢ) for discrete; x̄ = ∫x·dm / ∫dm for continuous mass distributions
Use similar triangles to relate the heights and distances of an object and its shadow; differentiate to find shadow length rate
An interior point c where f'(c) = 0 (horizontal tangent) or f'(c) does not exist (corner, cusp, or vertical tangent)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Center of mass (1D)" is defined as: x̄ = (Σmᵢxᵢ)/(Σmᵢ) for discrete; x̄ = ∫x·dm / ∫dm for continuous mass distributions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Hydrostatic force"?

options.text: Local min: concave up valley (f'' > 0); local max: concave down hill (f'' < 0)
A point where concavity changes; f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND the sign of f''(x) changes
Reading the y-value a function approaches by tracing the curve from both sides toward x = c; the function need not be defined at c
F = ∫₀ᴴ ρg·w(y)·y dy; force on a submerged vertical plate, where w(y) is width at depth y

quiz_questions.explanation: "Hydrostatic force" is defined as: F = ∫₀ᴴ ρg·w(y)·y dy; force on a submerged vertical plate, where w(y) is width at depth y. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "W = ∫ₐᵇ F(x)dx; the integral of force over displacement gives total work done (in joules or foot-pounds)"?

options.text: Second derivative
Zero derivative implies constant
d/dx[ln x] = 1/x
Work by variable force

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Work by variable force".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F = kx for a spring; work to stretch from 0 to x: W = ∫₀ˣ kx dx = ½kx²"?

options.text: Speed vs. velocity
Angular rate problems
Simplify before differentiating
Hooke's Law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Hooke's Law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "W = ∫₀ᴴ ρgA(y)·y dy; the work to pump water from a tank, where A(y) is the cross-sectional area at height y"?

options.text: Linear approximation
d/dx[sin x] = cos x
Interval additivity
Pumping work

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Pumping work".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "x̄ = (Σmᵢxᵢ)/(Σmᵢ) for discrete; x̄ = ∫x·dm / ∫dm for continuous mass distributions"?

options.text: d/dx[cos x] = -sin x
Fundamental Theorem (Part 1)
Center of mass (1D)
Implicit differentiation with time

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Center of mass (1D)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F = ∫₀ᴴ ρg·w(y)·y dy; force on a submerged vertical plate, where w(y) is width at depth y"?

options.text: Calculator vs. non-calculator
Net change interpretation
Hydrostatic force
Displacement vs. distance

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Hydrostatic force".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Applications to Physics is accurate?

options.text: Then solve algebraically for dy/dx.
Applications to Physics uses integration to solve problems in mechanics and other physics domains.
The power rule: d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n.
Integration by parts may need to be applied more than once.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Applications to Physics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Applications to Physics?

options.text: Work done by a variable force: W = ∫ₐᵇ F(x)dx.
Integration by parts may need to be applied more than once.
Energy conservation connects work to kinetic and potential energy changes.
A key AP skill: translating between rate graphs, accumulation functions, and verbal descriptions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Applications to Physics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Applications to Physics?

options.text: Riemann sums approximate this area using rectangles: left, right, and midpoint sums with n subintervals.
Hooke's Law spring work: W = ∫₀ˣ kx dx = kx²/2.
The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects differentiation and integration: d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t)dt] = f(x).
Introduction to Integrals presents integration as the reverse of differentiation and as the accumulation of quantities.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Applications to Physics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Applications to Physics?

options.text: Energy conservation connects work to kinetic and potential energy changes.
Antiderivatives: Inverse Trigonometric Forms produces inverse trig functions from specific integrand patterns.
Center of mass: x̄ = (∫ₐᵇ x·ρ(x)dx)/(∫ₐᵇ ρ(x)dx).
When k > 0: exponential growth (population, investment); when k < 0: exponential decay (radioactive decay, drug clearance).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Applications to Physics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Applications to Physics?

options.text: These applications demonstrate how integration computes total effects of continuously varying quantities.
The fundamental pair: d/dx[sin x] = cos x and d/dx[cos x] = -sin x (derived from the limit definition using lim(h→0) sin(h)/h = 1).
Basic antiderivative rules: ∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (n ≠ -1), ∫eˣdx = eˣ + C, ∫(1/x)dx = ln|x| + C, ∫cos x dx = sin x + C, ∫sin x dx = -cos x + C.
Riemann sums approximate this area using rectangles: left, right, and midpoint sums with n subintervals.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Applications to Physics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Applications to Physics?

options.text: Dam engineering: computing hydrostatic pressure and total force on a dam face using integration of pressure over the submerged area
Temperature modeling: since temperature changes continuously, the IVT guarantees that between any two measured temperatures, every intermediate value was achieved
Water tank problems: given inflow rate and outflow rate functions, compute water level at any time t and find when the tank is fullest
Aquarium design: the total force on viewing windows increases dramatically with depth, requiring thicker glass for deeper tanks

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Applications to Physics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Applications to Physics?

options.text: Moore's Law: the roughly exponential growth of transistor density in microchips (doubling every ~2 years) eventually creating unforeseen limits in physics
Compound interest: computing d/dn[(1 + r/n)^(nt)] using logarithmic differentiation to analyze how compounding frequency affects growth
Aerospace: calculating the work needed to launch a satellite by integrating the gravitational force F = GMm/r² from Earth's surface to orbit
Aquarium design: the total force on viewing windows increases dramatically with depth, requiring thicker glass for deeper tanks

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Applications to Physics.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Applications to Physics applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Orbital mechanics: computing orbital periods and trajectories involves integrals with expressions like √(a²-x²), solved with trigonometric substitution
Computer-aided design (CAD): finding normal vectors to complex curved surfaces defined implicitly requires combined differentiation techniques
Long-term drug concentration models: horizontal asymptotes represent the steady-state drug level that the body approaches with regular dosing
Automotive suspension design: computing the work done compressing a non-linear spring (where F(x) is not simply kx) for ride comfort optimization

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Applications to Physics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Applications to Physics?

options.text: Compound interest: computing d/dn[(1 + r/n)^(nt)] using logarithmic differentiation to analyze how compounding frequency affects growth
Population modeling: P(t) = P₀e^(rt), so P'(t) = rP₀e^(rt) = rP(t), showing exponential growth rate is proportional to population size
Velocity as the derivative of position: v(t) = s'(t) tells you the instantaneous speed and direction of a moving object at any moment
Dam engineering: computing hydrostatic pressure and total force on a dam face using integration of pressure over the submerged area

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Applications to Physics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Applications to Physics in practice?

options.text: Navigation: calculating bearing angles using arctan(Δy/Δx) and differentiating to find rate of angle change for tracking systems
Algorithm analysis in computer science: O(n log n) sorting algorithms (merge sort) outperform O(n²) algorithms (bubble sort) for large datasets
Aerospace: calculating the work needed to launch a satellite by integrating the gravitational force F = GMm/r² from Earth's surface to orbit
Oil spill spreading: a circular oil slick expanding on water has dA/dt = 2πr·dr/dt, used to estimate cleanup time and containment needs

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Applications to Physics.
